body_txt: In Middle Eastern folklore, the jinn are a species of spiritual beings with capabilities far surpassing that of humans. Some of the greatest magicians in Arabic lore were able to capture jinn to their service, and tied them to items such as bottles (specifically qamaqim ), lamps, or rings. Jinn were not usually obligated to give wishes to whoever helped them; if they did, it would be out of gratitude or an incentive to open the bottle. Some jinn got impatient and settled for just leaving if freed, while others took revenge on humanity by killing/tormenting whatever unlucky human released them . If they were bound to their prison, jinn were slaves to their owners but did not have any reality warping powers per se. After all, the "wish" was more like a command , and the jinn simply used their incredible powers to do their master's bidding. If their master wished for a castle, they built one (with varying levels of efficiency: a powerful jinni may do it in seconds whereas a weaker jinni may spend years on the task). If they wanted money, the jinni pulled it out of their own coffers (a human's mind being unable to comprehend how much they had). Nevertheless, a jinni was still allowed to refuse orders if their master asked for something beyond their abilities. Notions of jinn , their ability to fulfill wishes, and their captivity in everyday objects reached the Western World largely through Tales of One Thousand and One Nights . In particular " Aladdin " followed at a distance by "The Fisherman and the Jinni" shaped Western perception with little regard for the greater cultural, let alone religious, context. As such, jinn in Western works are most often found within antiquated brass oil lamps as per Aladdin . Most Western viewers (but not the characters ), upon seeing this kind of lamp, immediately associate it with a jinni . Many depictions show them living in the lamp, rather than being trapped there against their will. As per the original stories, Jinn are summoned either by opening the bottle or via rubbing the lamp or ring. They may offer to grant wishes unto the person who freed them. These wishes can be anything (although some give rule-based limitations ). A Benevolent Genie will attempt to fulfill the spirit of the master's wish. Less lucky characters may end up with a Literal Genie , who will follow your wish's Exact Words , which usually means that it will backfire if not worded carefully. A malevolent jinni is a Jackass Genie , and will fulfill the worst possible interpretation, no matter how carefully you attempt to word it. Your only hope with such a jinni is to either not make wishes at all or attempt to cause a Wishplosion , though this, too, can potentially backfire. Typically, jinn who do their best to follow their master's true wish will tend to fall into the hands of villains who will exploit them egregiously . The wishes that jinn grant have become a kind of Reality Warper that requires a human master. A jinni can do basically anything, up to reshaping the world and rewriting history, but need a master figure to request this of them first. They may have powers they can use themselves, but nowhere near what they can do if a human says "I wish..." first. This often seems to be a function of the lamp, as a freed jinn may not be able to grant wishes even if they want to, or will only be able to grant lesser wishes. Most modern depictions of jinn have a rule that they can only give their master Three Wishes (and ixnay on the Wishing for More Wishes !). If this is the case, expect a none-too-bright master to waste the first one or two on pointless fripperies before learning their lesson and using the third to make some meaningful change to their lives. Alternatively, their first two wishes may have Gone Horribly Wrong (whether from Exact Words , a Jackass Genie , or just poorly thought-out wishes), and the third will be used to undo them . The other common use for the third wish is Freeing the Genie , particularly if they are a Benevolent Genie . This one is well enough known that Christina Aguilera 's first song was called "Genie In A Bottle" and featured many innuendoes based on this trope. Do not confuse with Fairy in a Bottle . Subtrope of Our Genies Are Different . May overlap with Sealed Good in a Can or Sealed Evil in a Can , depending on the genie's personality.
